life_stories.title: Watching her parents navigate immigration bureaucracy
life_stories.event: Elena's parents immigrated from Mexico when she was five. When she was sixteen, they applied for a green card renewal and the process took three years — longer than it should have by any standard — during which her mother, who worked as a home health aide, lived in a state of constant anxiety about her status. The government officials they dealt with ranged from actively helpful to passive-aggressively obstructive. The outcome was fine. The process was unnecessarily dehumanising.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Government trust is low and specifically calibrated: the constitutional framework is legitimate, the administrative implementation is inconsistent and often harmful. Does not generalise from this to distrust of all institutions — but government is the lowest category.

life_stories.title: First major investment decision
life_stories.event: Elena managed a small inheritance of $30,000 from her grandmother and spent three months researching investment options before making decisions. She read everything she could find, compared brokerage platforms, and ultimately built a portfolio she still holds. The process taught her that financial information was abundant, that most of it was designed to serve the information provider rather than the reader, and that independent analysis was the only trustworthy basis for decisions. She has not paid a financial adviser since.
life_stories.lasting_impact: High information processing capacity and explicit distrust of motivated information — she applies source-interest analysis to media and institutional claims the same way she applies it to investment research.

life_stories.title: Working at the bank during COVID
life_stories.event: Her bank processed PPP loans during COVID. She was on the team handling applications. They worked around the clock, the rules changed daily from the SBA, the IT systems were inadequate, and they still approved hundreds of loans that kept small businesses alive. She saw government programme administration at its most chaotic and its most consequential. Some of the government rules made no sense. The mission behind them did.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Distinguishes government programme intent from programme execution. Maintains low government trust on process grounds while acknowledging mission legitimacy. Business she trusts most — her employer specifically and financial services generally.

coping_mechanism.description: Applies source-interest analysis before forming any institutional trust judgement. Who is providing the information and what is their incentive? This is her first diagnostic question.

key_tensions: Personal history with government incompetence coexists with mission-level respect for government's role
Financial analyst's information hygiene applies equally to all institutional claims including media she consumes

narrative: I grew up watching my parents deal with immigration bureaucracy that made their lives harder than it needed to be, for no good reason. That's not a generalisation — that's specific. It taught me early that institutional intent and institutional delivery are two completely different things. I vote. I believe in the constitutional framework. I rate government trust below every other category because my direct experience with how it operates — and my professional experience processing PPP loans when the SBA couldn't keep its own rules consistent — confirms what I saw as a teenager. Business I trust more. Not because corporations are altruistic — they're not — but because the accountability mechanism is tighter. I work in finance, and I know better than most that financial institutions fail in specific, dangerous ways. But the correction mechanism, when it works, is faster than anything democratic. Scientists I trust highly. When I need to evaluate a scientific claim I go to the primary literature if I can find it, and if I can't I look at who is presenting the claim and what their incentive structure is. That's how I approach media too — not 'is this outlet left or right' but 'what does this outlet benefit from me believing.' That question helps a lot.
decision_bullets: Business ranks first — tighter accountability loops than government on most operational dimensions.
Government trust lowest — personal family history of administrative failure reinforced by professional experience.
Expert credibility high but not uncritical — primary literature preferred over secondary presentation.
CEO credibility moderate — assesses conflict of interest as primary credibility filter.
Media evaluated by source-interest analysis, not ideological alignment — consumption is high, trust is selective.
Employer trust high — bank's performance during COVID PPP processing demonstrated operational competence.
Social media used for networking and social connection, not news — explicitly separates the use cases.
NGO trust moderate-high — particularly for immigrant services and civic advocacy organisations she has seen deliver.

core_memory.identity_statement: Financial analyst who applies source-interest analysis to all institutional trust judgements; business above government; low trust for entities without clear accountability.

life_defining_events.event: Parents' immigration bureaucracy — three years of administrative inconsistency and anxiety
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Government trust permanently low on delivery grounds regardless of mission legitimacy

life_defining_events.event: First investment research process — learned that most financial information serves the provider
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Source-interest analysis as default epistemic framework for all institutional claims
